mnemonics: Projection Formula: 'P is for Parent, U is for Uncle'. Think: To project vector 'v' onto its 'parent' line (spanned by 'u'), you need the help of the 'uncle' (the inner product of v and u, divided by the norm of u squared). Formula: proj_u(v) = (⟨v, u⟩ / ⟨u, u⟩) * u.

Orthogonal Means Perpendicular: Remember that the error vector (v - proj_u(v)) is orthogonal to the projection vector. Imagine it forming a perfect right angle. If the dot/inner product is zero, they are orthogonal.

Pythagoras in Disguise: For an orthogonal projection, the norm of the original vector squared equals the norm of the projection squared plus the norm of the error squared: ||v||² = ||proj||² + ||error||². It's the Pythagorean theorem in n-dimensions!
keyPoints: The projection of a vector v onto a subspace W is the unique vector in W that is closest to v.
The difference between v and its projection onto W (the error vector) is orthogonal to every vector in W.
The formula for the projection onto a one-dimensional subspace (line) spanned by a vector u is: proj_u(v) = (⟨v, u⟩ / ⟨u, u⟩) * u.

To project onto a higher-dimensional subspace W with an orthonormal basis {u1, u2, ..., uk}, the formula is simply the sum of the individual projections: proj_W(v) = ⟨v, u1⟩u1 + ⟨v, u2⟩u2 + ... + ⟨v, uk⟩uk.
commonMistakes: Using the projection formula without first ensuring the basis vectors for the subspace are orthogonal (or orthonormal). The simple sum formula only works for orthogonal bases.
Forgetting to square the norm of the denominator vector in the one-dimensional projection formula. The formula is ⟨v, u⟩ / ||u||², not ⟨v, u⟩ / ||u||.
Confusing the projection of v onto u with the projection of u onto v. These are different unless u and v are scalar multiples of each other.
Misapplying the concept to non-inner product spaces. Projections and orthogonality are defined by the inner product, so they depend on which inner product you are using.
